hints: Compute E[X₁X₂]=3/20, and marginals give E[X₂]=1/2, E[X₁]=1/4.
solution: Normalizing, $\int_0^1\int_0^{x_2}c(1-x_2)\,dx_1dx_2=c\int_0^1 x_2(1-x_2)dx_2=\tfrac{c}{6}=1\Rightarrow c=6$. Then $\mathbb{E}[X_1X_2]=3\int_0^1 x_2^3(1-x_2)dx_2=\tfrac3{20}$. The marginals give $\mathbb{E}[X_2]=\tfrac12$ and $f_{X_1}(x_1)=3(1-x_1)^2$ so $\mathbb{E}[X_1]=\tfrac14$. Hence $\mathrm{Cov}=\tfrac3{20}-\tfrac14\cdot\tfrac12=\tfrac1{40}.$

hints: Let x equal the whole expression; it appears again inside.
solution: Let $x=\sqrt{6+\sqrt{6+\cdots}}$, so $x=\sqrt{6+x}$, giving $x^2-x-6=0=(x-3)(x+2)$. Taking the positive root, $x=3.$

hints: Work in miles per minute: with wind r+x=1/3, against r−x=1/4.
solution: Let $r$ be his speed and $x$ the wind, in miles/min. Then $r+x=\tfrac13$ and $r-x=\tfrac14$, so $2r=\tfrac7{12}$, $r=\tfrac7{24}$. With no wind one mile takes $\tfrac1r=\tfrac{24}{7}$ minutes.

hints: Cover the units gap with 1s, then 5s, then 20s, then a 100.
solution: Use four $\$1$ bills (subset sums $0$–$4$), three $\$5$ bills (extending to $0$–$19$), four $\$20$ bills (to $0$–$99$), and one $\$100$. Every value $1$–$100$ is a subset sum, and this uses $4+3+4+1=12$ bills.
Require $1-0.9^n\ge0.9$, i.e. $0.9^n\le0.1$, so $n\ge\dfrac{\ln0.1}{\ln0.9}\approx21.85$. The smallest integer is $n=22.$

hints: The span from C's birth to B's death is 129 years; combined lifespans total 100, so there's a 29-year gap where neither lived.
Thus B was born 29 years after C died (the lives don't overlap).
C died 30 BC, so B was born 29 years later = 1 BC ⇒ −1.
solution: From C's birth to B's death is $129$ years, while their lifespans total $100$, leaving a $29$-year gap with neither alive — so C died, then $29$ years passed, then B was born. C died in $30$ BC, so B was born $29$ years later, in $1$ BC, i.e. $-1.$

hints: OLS slope a = Cov(X,Y)/Var(X) = 0 since Cov=0.
solution: With $\mathrm{Cov}=0$, the OLS slope is $a=\dfrac{\mathrm{Cov}(X,Y)}{\mathrm{Var}(X)}=0$, and the intercept is $b=\mathbb{E}[Y]-a\,\mathbb{E}[X]=8$. So $a+b=8.$

hints: Total deals of 12 distinct cards into four hands of 3: 12!/(3!)⁴.
Favorable: assign the 4 of each rank one-per-player: (4!)³.
solution: There are $\dfrac{12!}{(3!)^4}=369600$ ways to deal four distinct hands of $3$. For each player to get one K, one Q, one J, assign each rank's four cards one per player: $(4!)^3=13824$ ways. So $P=\dfrac{13824}{369600}=\dfrac{72}{1925}.$

hints: A bid exceeds 9000 with probability e^{−9000/5000}=e^{−9/5}.
The number of bids until the first success is Geom(p), mean 1/p.
solution: Each bid exceeds $\$9000$ with probability $p=e^{-9000/5000}=e^{-9/5}$. The number of bids until the first such bid is geometric with mean $1/p=e^{9/5}$, so $a=\tfrac95.$

hints: For a Brownian bridge, Cov(X_s,X_t)=s(1−t) for s≤t.
solution: The Brownian bridge has $\mathrm{Cov}(X_s,X_t)=s(1-t)$ for $s\le t$, so $\mathbb{E}[X_s\mid X_t=v]=v\dfrac{s(1-t)}{t(1-t)}=v\dfrac st$. With $v=3,s=\tfrac12,t=\tfrac34$: $\mathbb{E}=3\cdot\dfrac{1/2}{3/4}=2.$

hints: G~U(30,60), S~U(0,60); meeting iff |G−S|≤10. Sample area = 30·60 = 1800.
solution: With $G\sim U(30,60)$, $S\sim U(0,60)$, the meeting occurs iff $|G-S|\le10$ over a sample rectangle of area $1800$. For $G\in[30,50]$ the feasible $S$-length is $20$; for $G\in[50,60]$ it is $70-G$. Favorable area $=20\cdot20+\int_{50}^{60}(70-G)\,dG=400+150=550$, so $P=\tfrac{550}{1800}=\tfrac{11}{36}.$

hints: Consider how a call's value behaves as σ grows without bound.
Write the call as S·Φ(d₁) − Ke^{−rT}Φ(d₂) and let σ→∞.
Examine what Φ(d₁) and Φ(d₂) tend to in that limit.
